items.q: How should you express an opinion in the speaking exam?
items.correct: Give the opinion and justify it, e.g. 'à mon avis... parce que...'
items.misconception: State a one-word answer with no reason
items.why: Students give bare opinions; justifying with reasons and examples gains far higher marks for spontaneous talk.

items.q: Which form of address suits a formal speaking exam?
items.why: Students use 'tu' with the examiner; the formal 'vous' is expected in this register.

items.q: What helps make spoken answers flow?
items.why: Students list ideas without linking them; connectives signal contrast and consequence and raise the mark.

items.q: Which phrase best introduces a justified opinion in a speaking exam?
items.random_wrong: Je ne sais pas du tout.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: À mon avis, ... parce que .... “The end.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why are connectives like 'cependant' and 'par conséquent' useful when speaking?
items.correct: They link ideas and make arguments flow logically
items.random_wrong: They are only used in maths
items.why: The answer examiners want is: They link ideas and make arguments flow logically. “They make speech shorter” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What shows good spontaneity in the oral exam?
items.correct: Responding naturally to unexpected follow-up questions
items.misconception: Reciting a fixed script regardless of the question
items.why: It's Responding naturally to unexpected follow-up questions. “Reciting a fixed script regardless of the question” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
